title: Rough-puff pastry
subtitle: The home baker's laminated shortcut — 700 folds of flaky layers
excerpt: Four hundred grams of plain flour, cold butter cut into large chunks (not rubbed in), iced water to bind. Four folds over two hours gives a pastry that lifts to visible, flaky layers without the precision of classic puff.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: puff pastry chapters covering the fold-and-rest principle; rough puff is described as the household economy version. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg: short crust and puff variants with precise weight ratios. Florence White, Good Things in England (1932): rough-puff documented as the standard home pastry for savoury pies and meat pasties. Mrs Rundell, A New System of Domestic Cookery (1806), Project Gutenberg: early fold-and-rest pastry instructions where the cold rest between folds was already understood as the method's core.

recipe.yieldDescription: about 500 g rough-puff (enough for one 23 cm tart lid or base, or a batch of eight sausage rolls)
recipe.freezeNotes: Wrap the uncooked pastry block tightly in cling film and freeze for up to 2 months. Defrost overnight in the fridge before rolling.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make the pastry the day before; it keeps in the fridge for 2 days. The rest overnight improves the gluten relaxation.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; the actual bake temperature depends on what the pastry is being used for

recipeTools.notes: A marble slab keeps the butter cool. A cold worktop works too.
For folding and lifting the dough without warming it.
The bake temperature is critical for laminated pastry.

glossaryTerms.definition: The process of folding butter into a dough repeatedly to create distinct layers. Each fold multiplies the existing layers; after four folds the dough has over 700 alternating layers of butter and flour. Steam from the butter during baking pushes the layers apart.

content.text: Rough-puff pastry sits between shortcrust and full puff. The butter is cut into large pieces and mixed cold into the flour without fully rubbing in — large butter chunks remain visible. Rolling and folding those chunks creates the layers; the rest periods between folds keep the butter firm enough to stay distinct rather than blending into the flour. The result lifts to visible, flaky layers that a shortcrust never produces, without the precision of a full puff lamination.

The method's one firm requirement is cold. Cold butter, cold water, a cold working surface, and the discipline to put the dough back in the fridge whenever it feels warm or sticky. Warm butter melts into the flour during rolling, which produces a dense pastry and no layers.

attrs.title: Butter temperature
attrs.body: The butter should feel cold and firm when you cut it. Take it straight from the fridge. If your kitchen is warm and the butter starts to soften during mixing, put the bowl in the freezer for 5 minutes before you continue.

content.text: What you need

items.prepNote: ice cold (add a few ice cubes to the jug, pour from above the cubes)

content.text: The fat-to-flour ratio is 75% — higher than shortcrust (50%) but lower than full puff (100%). Strong bread flour produces a tougher pastry; stick to plain flour for a tender, flaky result. Salted butter is fine; reduce the salt to a pinch if using it.
Method
Mix the dough

Weigh {{plain-flour}} plain flour and {{sea-salt-fine}} fine sea salt into the large bowl and mix briefly. Add {{unsalted-butter}} cold butter cubes. Using a round-bladed knife, cut the butter through the flour until the cubes are roughly halved — about a dozen cuts. Some cubes will be flat, some still blocky; none should be fully rubbed in. This roughness is what you want.

Pour in most of the {{water}} ice-cold water and mix quickly with the knife until the dough just comes together in rough clumps. Add more water by the tablespoon if the dough won't collect. Bring it together briefly with your hands — it should look shaggy and uneven with visible butter pieces throughout. Don't press it smooth; it shouldn't be.
Chill the dough, 30 minutes
Shape the dough into a rough rectangle, wrap in cling film, and refrigerate for 30 minutes. The rest allows the gluten to relax and the butter to firm up again before rolling.
First fold

On a lightly floured surface, roll the dough into a rectangle roughly 45 × 15 cm. Keep the rolling even and deliberate; press firmly enough to flatten the butter without tearing the dough. Fold the bottom third up and the top third down, as you would a letter. You now have a block about 15 × 15 cm. Turn it 90° so the open edge faces right. This is the first fold of the
lamination
. Wrap and refrigerate for 30 minutes. The dough should feel firm again after the rest; if butter is visible at the edges, that is correct.
Second fold — rest 30 minutes

Remove from the fridge and roll again to 45 × 15 cm. Fold and turn as before. Wrap and refrigerate for another 30 minutes. After the second fold the dough begins to look smoother, with fewer visible butter lumps; this is correct progress.
Third fold — rest 30 minutes

Roll, fold, turn, and chill again for 30 minutes. By now the dough should feel cohesive and rollable without tearing. Resist the urge to skip the rest; skipping it warms the butter and collapses the forming layers.
Fourth fold — final rest 30 minutes

Complete the fourth fold and return to the fridge for a minimum of 30 minutes before rolling out to use. After the four folds the dough has over 700 alternating layers of butter and flour. If you see obvious butter streaks at the cut edge before the final rest, that is fine; after baking, those streaks are the layers.

attrs.title: Using the pastry
attrs.body: Roll chilled rough-puff to the thickness your recipe needs — typically 4–5 mm for a pie lid or base, 3 mm for sausage rolls. Bake at 200°C fan for most applications. The bake temperature and time depend on the filling; the pastry itself is ready when it is deep golden and the layers have opened at the cut edges.

content.text: Troubleshooting

attrs.heading: When things go sideways
attrs.intro: The three most common problems with rough-puff.

items.cause: Butter melted into the flour during mixing or rolling; the layers never formed.
items.fix: Keep everything cold. If the butter is soft when it comes out of the fridge, return it for another 10 minutes. Work quickly during rolling; put the dough back in the fridge if it starts to feel warm or sticky.

items.cause: Too little water (dough won't hold together) or gluten hasn't relaxed enough.
items.fix: Ensure the rest periods are at least 30 minutes. If the dough tears after resting, it may need slightly more water; gather the torn pieces back together and press them before returning to the fridge.

items.symptom: Pastry shrinks dramatically in the oven
items.cause: Dough was stretched during rolling rather than rolled evenly; gluten under tension snaps back on heat.
items.fix: Roll with even, steady pressure rather than pushing the pin away from you. Let the dough rest 10 minutes after rolling out to its final shape, before cutting or placing into the tin.

content.text: Where this pastry sits

Rough-puff is the practical British kitchen version of the French mille-feuille tradition. Mrs Beeton described it as the household economy method in 1861: full puff required a marble slab, considerable time, and a consistent cold. Rough-puff asks for the same rests but tolerates a less precise kitchen. Florence White records it as standard for savoury pies across regional Britain; Mrs Acton's 1845 weighting is the foundation the recipe builds from.
